blocks.text: The wise will not hate the law, but the one who is hypocritical about it is like a boat in a storm.
The sensible person will trust in the law; for such a one the law is as dependable as a divine oracle.
3 : , likely the Urim and Thummin (45.10; Ex 28.30; Lev 8.8; Num 27.21; 1 Sam 14.41–42).
Prepare what to say, and then you will be listened to; draw upon your training, and give your answer.
The heart of a fool is like a cart wheel, and his thoughts like a turning axle.
A mocking friend is like a stallion that neighs no matter who the rider is.
32.14–33.6: Deliberate judgments.
Why is one day more important than another, when all the daylight in the year is from the sun?
By the Lord's wisdom they were distinguished, and he appointed the different seasons and festivals.
Some days he exalted and hallowed, and some he made ordinary days.
7–9 : Sabbath and liturgical festivals (cf. Gen 1.14; 2.3).
All human beings come from the ground, and humankind was created out of the dust.
In the fullness of his knowledge the Lord distinguished them and appointed their different ways.
Some he blessed and exalted, and some he made holy and brought near to himself; but some he cursed and brought low, and turned them out of their place.
12 : , figures such as Abraham and Moses. , a likely reference to the priesthood. , perhaps referring to the Canaanites (cf. 16.9).
Like clay in the hand of the potter, to be molded as he pleases, so all are in the hand of their Maker, to be given whatever he decides.
Good is the opposite of evil, and life the opposite of death; so the sinner is the opposite of the godly.
14 : , for prayer (see 39.5).
Look at all the works of the Most High; they come in pairs, one the opposite of the other.
33.7–15: The pairs. God created the cosmos in pairs (v. 15), an order that can be observed and extends to humans, as the wise and foolish or the and the (v. 14).
Now I was the last to keep vigil; I was like a gleaner following the grape-pickers;
16 : , a prophetic image; see Ezek 33.7; Hab 2.1 (cf. Jer 1.12; 31.28; 44.27 for the same verb). collects what remains after the harvest; see Deut 24.19–22.
by the blessing of the Lord I arrived first, and like a grape-picker I filled my wine press.
16–17 : , perhaps a reference to the sage in an official capacity (cf. 38.31–33). , the contrasts with those .
Consider that I have not labored for myself alone, but for all who seek instruction.
Hear me, you who are great among the people, and you leaders of the congregation, pay heed!
33.16–19: Ben Sira the sage. For other authorial self-references, see 24.30–34; 34.9–13; 39.12–13; 50.27.
To son or wife, to brother or friend, do not give power over yourself, as long as you live; and do not give your property to another, in case you change your mind and must ask for it.
While you are still alive and have breath in you, do not let anyone take your place.
For it is better that your children should ask from you than that you should look to the hand of your children.
Excel in all that you do; bring no stain upon your honor.
At the time when you end the days of your life, in the hour of death, distribute your inheritance.
20–24 : Loss of control over property and family brought a .
Fodder and a stick and burdens for a donkey; bread and discipline and work for a slave.
Set your slave to work, and you will find rest; leave his hands idle, and he will seek liberty.
Yoke and thong will bow the neck, and for a wicked slave there are racks and tortures.
Put him to work, in order that he may not be idle,
for idleness teaches much evil.
Set him to work, as is fitting for him, and if he does not obey, make his fetters heavy. Do not be overbearing toward anyone, and do nothing unjust.
If you have but one slave, treat him like yourself, because you have bought him with blood. If you have but one slave, treat him like a brother, for you will need him as you need your life.
31 : , the high cost of purchasing a slave justified good treatment.
If you ill-treat him, and he leaves you and runs away,
which way will you go to seek him?
33.20–33: The household.
25–33 : Slavery (cf. 7.20–21) was a sanctioned institution, which was regulated in Israelite law (Ex 21.2–11,20–21,26–27; Lev 25.39–46; Deut 15.12–18).
